stimuli.description: Red stop-signal arrow (stop_left.png or stop_right.png) presented at SSD ms after go-arrow onset; correct response is withhold.
White go-arrow (go_left.png or go_right.png); respond ArrowLeft for left, ArrowRight for right.

phase_detection.complete: !!(document.body && document.body.innerText && document.body.innerText.indexOf('Thank you for your participation')>=0)

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: results section reporting posterior ex-Gaussian estimates for go-trial RTs across four datasets
citations.quote: Posterior means for the ex-Gaussian mu parameter on go trials ranged across datasets in approximately the 400-460 ms window in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Van Zandt, T., Verbruggen, F., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: General and special theories of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: summary of go-RT central tendencies across cited stop-signal studies
citations.quote: Go-trial mean RTs in two-choice stop-signal tasks typically fall in the 500-550 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: posterior estimates for sigma on go-trial RTs
citations.quote: Posterior estimates for sigma on go-trial RTs fell in roughly the 40-80 ms range across datasets.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Suess, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.table_or_figure: summary of ex-Gaussian sigma estimates in choice-RT tasks
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma estimates in two-choice tasks are typically clustered around 40-60 ms with substantial inter-individual variation.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: posterior estimates for tau on go-trial RTs
citations.quote: Tau estimates capturing the right-tail of go-trial RT distributions were on the order of 80-130 ms across datasets.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Suess, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.table_or_figure: summary of ex-Gaussian tau estimates in choice-RT tasks and links to cognitive abilities
citations.quote: Tau, the exponential component, varies more across individuals than mu or sigma and is linked to lapses of attention.

go.rationale: Ex-Gaussian fits to go-trial RTs in stop-signal tasks consistently yield mu ~380-430 ms, sigma ~40-60 ms, tau ~80-120 ms in healthy young adults, giving a mean go RT around 500-520 ms typical of STOP-IT and related two-choice stop-signal paradigms (Verbruggen & Logan lineage).

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: horse-race model formulation; signal-respond RT < no-signal RT prediction
citations.quote: The mean of the signal-respond RT distribution should be shorter than the mean of the no-signal RT distribution because only fast-finish go processes escape the stop process.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: comparison of failed-stop (signal-respond) and go-trial ex-Gaussian estimates
citations.quote: Failed-stop trial RTs are truncated relative to go-trial RTs, yielding shorter mu and tau estimates consistent with horse-race truncation.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: horse-race model prediction that signal-respond distribution is a left-truncation of the go distribution
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are a left-truncation of the go-RT distribution; variability is reduced relative to the untruncated go distribution.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: horse-race prediction that the slow tail is preferentially inhibited
citations.quote: The slow tail of the go-RT distribution is preferentially inhibited, reducing the exponential component of signal-respond RTs relative to go RTs.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: tau estimates for signal-respond trials
citations.quote: Tau for signal-respond RTs was systematically smaller than tau for go RTs, consistent with truncation of the slow tail.

stop.rationale: Failed-stop (stop-respond) RTs are systematically shorter than go RTs per the independent horse-race model (Logan & Cowan, 1984): only fast-tail go trials escape inhibition. Mu and tau are reduced relative to the go distribution to reflect this truncation.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.table_or_figure: lag-1 autocorrelation summaries from serial choice-RT tasks
citations.quote: Short-range (lag-1) autocorrelations in serial choice-RT data are typically positive but modest, often in the 0.1-0.2 range.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: Section on temporal structure of serial RTs
citations.quote: Serial RTs exhibit weak but reliable short-range positive dependencies in addition to long-range 1/f structure.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-by-trial RT autocorrelation in serial choice-RT tasks is small but reliable (phi ~0.1-0.2); included to produce realistic short-range temporal dependencies in go-trial RTs.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: block-wise RT means showing modest within-session drift
citations.quote: Mean go-RTs change modestly across blocks in stop-signal sessions; magnitudes are small relative to block-to-block fluctuation.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across four experimental blocks (~250-300 trials), mild positive drift of ~0.1 ms/trial captures end-of-session slowing typical of speeded tasks; magnitude kept conservative because STOP-IT inserts 15 s block breaks that mitigate fatigue.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: review section enumerating documented sequential effects in stop-signal tasks
citations.quote: The principal documented sequential effects in stop-signal paradigms are post-stop slowing and post-error slowing; lag-1 go-condition repetition effects analogous to conflict-task congruency-sequence effects are not characteristic of the paradigm.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: review section enumerating documented sequential effects in stop-signal tasks
citations.quote: Lag-1 cost effects on go-trial RTs tied to go-direction repetition are not a documented finding in the stop-signal literature; the salient sequential effects are post-stop and post-error slowing.

condition_repetition.rationale: Stop-signal literature does not document reliable lag-1 condition-repetition facilitation/costs on go-trial RTs beyond what post_event_slowing (interrupt trigger) already captures.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: Spectral characterization of serial choice-RT fluctuations
citations.quote: Slow trial-to-trial fluctuations in serial RT contribute on the order of 10-25 ms of standard deviation beyond what would be expected from white noise.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: Estimated spectral slopes and corresponding Hurst exponents
citations.quote: Spectral slopes in serial choice-RT data correspond to Hurst exponents in the 0.65-0.85 range, indicative of persistent long-range fluctuations.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.table_or_figure: estimated 1/f^alpha exponents and corresponding Hurst values
citations.quote: Estimated long-range dependence exponents in cognitive time series correspond to Hurst values typically between 0.65 and 0.85.

pink_noise.rationale: Long-range RT fluctuations with Hurst exponent ~0.7-0.8 are well-documented in serial RT tasks (Gilden, 2001) and reproduce 1/f-like temporal structure independent of task-specific modulation.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: review of sequential effects in stop-signal paradigms
citations.quote: Beyond post-stop and post-error slowing, lag-1 prev-by-current pair modulations of go-trial RTs are not a reported finding in the stop-signal literature.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.table_or_figure: discussion of post-error trial handling in sequential analyses
citations.quote: When analyzing sequential effects, the trial immediately following an error is typically excluded from condition-pair sequential analyses because post-error slowing dominates that trial's RT.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Lag-1 prev x curr modulation (e.g., conflict-adaptation style) is a conflict-task phenomenon; the stop-signal literature does not document analogous condition-pair modulation of go-trial RTs.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: post-stop vs no-stop RT differences
citations.quote: Go-trial RTs following stop-signal trials are reliably slower than go-trial RTs following other go trials, with typical post-stop slowing of approximately 30-80 ms.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.table_or_figure: review of post-stop slowing magnitudes across studies
citations.quote: Post-stop-signal slowing is one of the most consistently observed sequential effects in the stop-signal paradigm, with magnitudes typically in the range of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.table_or_figure: trial-by-trial RT analyses around error trials
citations.quote: Responses following an error are systematically slower than responses following correct trials.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.table_or_figure: magnitudes of post-error slowing across studies and conditions
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in two-choice tasks is typically on the order of 10-60 ms when assessed at lag 1.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-stop-signal slowing on the trial following a stop trial is reliably observed (Bissett & Logan, 2011; Verbruggen et al., 2014) at typical magnitudes of 30-80 ms. Post-error slowing on go-trial choice errors is also documented (Rabbitt, 1966 lineage) at 20-60 ms; marked exclusive_with_prior_triggers so interrupt slowing dominates when both events would apply on adjacent trials.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Standard between-subject jitter for choice-RT paradigms (RT mean SD ~50 ms; per-condition SD ~15 ms; sigma/tau scaling +/-15%). accuracy_clip_range widened to [0.4, 0.99] because the stop condition's correct-withholding rate is by design ~50% (SSD staircase converges on ~50% inhibition); a tighter lower bound would clip plausible stop-condition variation across subjects.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Suess, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.table_or_figure: between-subject SDs of mean RT in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean RT in two-choice tasks in young adults are typically on the order of 40-80 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Suess, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.table_or_figure: between-subject SDs of condition-specific RT differences
citations.quote: Inter-individual variability in condition-specific RT shifts within a paradigm is substantially smaller than variability in overall mean RT, typically on the order of 10-25 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Suess, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.table_or_figure: inter-individual variability of ex-Gaussian sigma and tau
citations.quote: Individual estimates of ex-Gaussian sigma and tau in two-choice tasks span approximately +/-15-25% around population-typical values in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: go-trial accuracy descriptive statistics
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy in adult two-choice stop-signal tasks is typically near ceiling (>= 0.93) with modest between-subject SD on the order of 0.02-0.04.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: go-trial omission descriptive statistics
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates in healthy adult stop-signal samples are low (typically < 0.05) with between-subject SD around 0.01-0.02.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: staircase convergence section
citations.quote: Adaptive SSD tracking procedures converge on approximately 50% probability of successful inhibition on stop trials by design.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: summary of typical adult go-accuracy ranges
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy in adult samples typically lies above 0.90 and below ceiling; very low accuracies (< 0.5) indicate task non-compliance.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: go-trial omission descriptives and exclusion criteria
citations.quote: Studies commonly exclude participants whose go-trial omission rate exceeds approximately 10%, treating higher rates as indicative of task non-compliance.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'STOP-IT jsPsych Stop-Signal Task' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 2 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 33 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 27 parameters; 4 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 16 trials matched, conditions ['go', 'stop'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
